title: Set up the Library CLI project
instruction: A CLI app that manages a library catalog using everything you've learned. Define a `Book` struct with `Title`, `Author`, `ISBN`, and `Available` fields. Create an empty catalog in `main()` and print `"Welcome to the Library CLI"`.

codeChecks.message: Add Available bool field to Book.

successMessage: Your Library CLI is set up with a Book struct and a catalog map ready to hold books.

title: Add the addBook helper
instruction: Write a function `addBook(catalog map[string]Book, title, author, isbn string, available bool)` that adds a Book to the catalog map keyed by ISBN. Add one book to the catalog and print it with `fmt.Printf("%+v\n", catalog[isbn])`.
hint: func addBook(catalog map[string]Book, title, author, isbn string, available bool) { catalog[isbn] = Book{Title: title, Author: author, ISBN: isbn, Available: available} }

codeChecks.message: Add the book to the catalog map keyed by ISBN.
Print the added book using fmt.Printf.

successMessage: addBook stores a new book in the catalog by ISBN. Maps make lookups fast and easy.

title: Search books by title
instruction: Write `searchBooks(catalog map[string]Book, query string) []Book` that returns all books whose title contains the query (case-insensitive using `strings.Contains` and `strings.ToLower`). Search for a book and print the result count.
hint: func searchBooks(catalog map[string]Book, query string) []Book { var results []Book; for _, b := range catalog { if strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(b.Title), strings.ToLower(query)) { results = append(results, b) } }; return results }

codeChecks.message: Use range to iterate over the catalog.
Use append to build the results slice.

successMessage: Your library can search books by title. Case-insensitive search makes it user-friendly.

title: List all books
instruction: Write `listBooks(catalog map[string]Book)` that prints all books in a formatted table. Use `fmt.Printf("| %-30s | %-20s | %-15s | %-9s |\n", ...)` for alignment. Add 3 books and list them all.
hint: fmt.Printf("| %-30s | %-20s | %-15s | %-9s |\n", b.Title, b.Author, b.ISBN, fmt.Sprint(b.Available))  — add a header row first.

codeChecks.message: Use range to iterate over the catalog.

successMessage: A clean table of all books in the library. Formatting makes data readable at a glance.

title: Check out a book with error handling
instruction: Write `checkout(catalog map[string]Book, isbn string) error` that marks a book as unavailable. Return an error if the ISBN is not found or if the book is already checked out. Use `errors.New` for custom errors. Test both cases.
hint: book, ok := catalog[isbn]; if !ok { return errors.New("book not found") }; if !book.Available { return errors.New("book already checked out") }; book.Available = false; catalog[isbn] = book; return nil

codeChecks.message: Use errors.New for custom error messages.

successMessage: Two error cases handled: missing books and double checkouts. Robust library software needs both.

title: Return a book with validation
instruction: Write `returnBook(catalog map[string]Book, isbn string) error` that marks a checked-out book as available again. Return an error if the ISBN is not found or if the book was not checked out. Test returning an already-available book.
hint: if book.Available { return errors.New("book was not checked out") }; book.Available = true; catalog[isbn] = book; return nil

codeChecks.message: Return a specific error when trying to return an available book.
Set Available to true when returning.

successMessage: Check out and return with full validation. Your library tracks exactly which books are in circulation.

title: Count books by availability
instruction: Write `stats(catalog map[string]Book)` that prints: total books, available books, and checked out books. Use a loop with counters.
hint: total := len(catalog); for _, b := range catalog { if b.Available { avail++ } else { out++ } }

codeChecks.message: Use len(catalog) for the total count.
Check the Available field in your loop.

successMessage: Stats give you a birds-eye view of the library. Total, available, and checked-out counts at a glance.

title: Load sample data
instruction: Write `loadSampleData() map[string]Book` that creates a catalog with 5 pre-loaded books and returns it. Use `addBook` internally. Call it in main and list the books.
hint: func loadSampleData() map[string]Book { catalog := make(map[string]Book); addBook(catalog, ...); return catalog }
successMessage: Sample data lets you test and demonstrate the library without typing books by hand every time.

title: Interactive menu loop
instruction: Build an interactive CLI menu using `fmt.Scanln` and a `for` loop. Print options: 1. List books, 2. Search, 3. Checkout, 4. Return, 5. Stats, 0. Exit. Read the user's choice and call the matching function. Run it and test listing books.

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Scanln to read user input.
Use a switch on the user's choice.
Use an infinite for loop for the menu.

successMessage: An interactive CLI menu! Users can list, search, checkout, return, and view stats — all from the terminal.

title: Complete the CLI with all operations
instruction: Add the remaining menu options to the switch: `case 2` (Search — prompt for a query and print results), `case 3` (Checkout — prompt for ISBN), `case 4` (Return — prompt for ISBN). Test all operations by running the program.
hint: case 2: var q string; fmt.Print("Search: "); fmt.Scanln(&q); results := searchBooks(catalog, q); for _, b := range results { fmt.Printf("- %s by %s\n", b.Title, b.Author) }

codeChecks.message: Prompt the user and read input for search/checkout/return.

successMessage: Your Library CLI is complete! You built a working interactive CLI app using structs, maps, functions, error handling, and fmt — everything you've learned in this course.
